reasoning:
The threshold for a positive resolution is firmly set around 43 fatalities (10 * 4.25). Reaching 43 fatalities in a 30-day period in Bolivia is extremely rare and typically associated with major political crises. The total fatalities in the entire 360-day period preceding the forecast date were approximately 39. Hitting 43 in just 30 days would require a level of violence greater than the entire preceding year combined, and even higher than the peak of the 2019 crisis (which saw about 36 deaths over several weeks).

For the 2026-02-22 resolution date, the 30-day window is from Jan 23 to Feb 21. With 23 of these days already passed as of the forecast date (Feb 15) and no news of mass casualties (only some protests over fuel subsidies with minimal to no fatalities reported), the probability is practically zero (0.02).

For the 2026-03-17 resolution date, the 30-day window is from Feb 15 to Mar 16. While this is entirely in the future and carries more uncertainty, the conditions for an unprecedented spike in civil violence to result in 43+ deaths in a month are not present. Even if the current political and economic tensions (Morales vs. Arce, fuel subsidies) escalate, reaching the 43-fatality threshold remains highly unlikely based on historical base rates for Bolivian unrest. Therefore, the probability remains very low, bumping up slightly for the extended time horizon (0.03).
